vulnerability.title: Prevent Login to Accounts With Empty Password
vulnerability.description: If an account is configured for password authentication but does not have an assigned password, it may be possible to log into the account without authentication. Remove any instances of the nullok in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth to prevent logins with empty passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if [ -f /usr/bin/authselect ]; then if ! authselect check; then echo " authselect integrity check failed. Remediation aborted! This remediation could not be applied because an authselect profile was not selected or the selected profile is not intact. It is not recommended to manually edit the PAM files when authselect tool is available. In cases where the default authselect profile does not cover a specific demand, a custom authselect profile is recommended." exit 1 fi authselect enable-feature without-nullok authselect apply-changes -b else if grep -qP "^\s*auth\s+sufficient\s+pam_unix.so\s.*\bnullok\b" "/etc/pam.d/system-auth"; then sed -i -E --follow-symlinks "s/(.*auth.*sufficient.*pam_unix.so.*)\

triage_decision.reason: The remediation uses the standard 'authselect' tool on RHEL 9 to disable the 'nullok' feature. This is the vendor-recommended method for managing PAM configurations and is highly unlikely to cause system breakage or lockout, provided the authselect profile is intact.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential lockout if existing accounts rely on empty passwords for automated processes (though this is a security violation by design)

review_feedback: QA validation error: OpenRouter API error 503: {"error":{"message":"Provider returned error","code":503,"metadata":{"raw":"{\"error\":\"The model is currently overloaded. previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will resolve the vulnerability by removing the nullok parameter, but it uses a manual 'sed' approach on PAM files instead of the recommended 'authselect' tool. On modern RHEL-based systems, manual edits to /etc/pam.d/ files are overwritten by authselect, making the fix temporary and potentially inconsistent.
concerns: Manual editing of PAM files via sed can be overwritten by the authselect tool on RHEL/CentOS/Fedora systems.
Manual edits risk corrupting the PAM configuration if the regex is too broad.
suggested_improvements: Use 'authselect enable-feature without-nullok' followed by 'authselect apply-changes' as recommended in the vulnerability description to ensure the change is persistent and follows system standards.
Verify the current authselect profile before making manual changes.
